title: Quantum Spin Squeezing Enhanced by Critical Exceptional Points
abstract: Critical exceptional points (CEPs) are nonequilibrium critical points in open many-body systems at which multiple collective excitation modes coalesce. CEPs are known to amplify classical fluctuations, but their effect on genuinely \textit{quantum} fluctuations remains unclear. Here, we show that dissipative collective-spin systems hosting CEPs exhibit parametrically enhanced steady-state \textit{quantum} spin squeezing. Close to the CEP, the optimally squeezed variance scales as $|Z|$, whereas the anti-squeezed variance diverges as $|Z|^{-1}$, with $Z$ the dimensionless order parameter. Importantly, the anti-squeezed fluctuation direction asymptotically aligns with the coalescing eigenvector of the stability matrix, reflecting the defective nature of the CEP dynamics. These scalings are robust against dephasing channels generated by spin components orthogonal to the coalesced critical collective mode. Our results identify CEPs as a route to engineering steady-state anisotropic quantum fluctuations and correlations in driven-dissipative platforms.
summary: This paper demonstrates that Critical Exceptional Points in open many-body systems can significantly enhance quantum spin squeezing. It establishes specific power-law scaling for squeezed and anti-squeezed variances near the transition and identifies the conditions under which this effect is robust against decoherence.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether Critical Exceptional Points (CEPs) in open many-body systems can be used to parametrically enhance quantum spin squeezing and how these effects scale near the transition.
main_result: Near a CEP, the optimally squeezed variance scales linearly with the order parameter (|Z|), while the anti-squeezed variance diverges (|Z|^{-1}), creating a highly anisotropic fluctuation plane. This enhancement is robust against certain dephasing channels but is destroyed by z-axis dephasing.
method: The study uses a mean-field approach, linearized dynamics around the PT-broken fixed point, and the solution of the steady-state Lyapunov equation within a Gaussian approximation in the thermodynamic limit.
model_or_system: The model consists of dissipative collective-spin systems governed by the GKSL master equation, specifically focusing on L-PT symmetric models such as a spin-boson system where a collective spin is coupled to a lossy cavity mode.
key_observables: Quantum spin squeezing parameter (Kitagawa-Ueda), Wineland parameter, covariance matrix, and the Husimi-Q distribution.
important_parameters: Dimensionless order parameter (Z), spin size (S), collective decay rate (kappa), transverse field (g), and dephasing rates (gamma).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the thermodynamic limit (large S), a Gaussian description of fluctuations, and specific constraints on the diffusion matrix (e.g., Dz = 0 for the primary scaling result).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of mode coalescence at the CEP and an illustration of the increased anisotropy of the spin distribution on the Bloch sphere. Figure 2 demonstrates the scaling collapse of the inverse squeezing using a logarithmically corrected finite-size scaling form.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of CEPs and their role in fluctuations, develops the mathematical framework for the L-PT symmetric collective spin model, derives the scaling laws for squeezing and anti-squeezing near the CEP, analyzes the robustness against dephasing, and concludes with finite-size scaling and experimental realizations.
why_interesting: It provides a novel mechanism for engineering highly anisotropic quantum correlations and steady-state entanglement by leveraging non-Hermitian singularities (CEPs) in driven-dissipative platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Top: schematic of collective excitation modes for the control parameter δ. The two linear collective modes (ar- rows) approach each other and coalesce at the CEP (pink) into a single defective direction. Bottom: schematic illustration of the steady-state spin distribution on the Bloch sphere. Near the CEP, the distribution becomes strongly anisotropic and elon- gated along the defective direction, whereas away from the CEP it becomes progressively more isotropic.

Figure 2. Numerical calculation of the dissipative collective spin model (10). (a) Order parameter ⟨mz⟩and (b) Kitagawa Ueda spin-squeezing parameter ξ2 S as functions of δ := (κ −κc)/ω in the steady-state, with g/ω = 2 . Colored solid lines are finite-S numerics, while the black dotted curve shows the large-S prediction. The pink marker indicates the CEP. (c-1)–(c-3) Husimi Q function of the steady state on the tangent plane orthogonal to the mean spin for the three values of δ = 0, 0.1, 1.0 for S = 500. Figure 2 (f) shows this collapse by plotting G(δ;S )S −1/3 eff against δ S 2/3 eff . The inset displays the un- scaled data, where the enhancement of G grows and shifts toward the CEP as S increases. In the rescaled plot, the curves for different S fall onto a common scaling function, including the location and height of the maximum. This agreement supports the logarithmically corrected finite- size scaling in Eq. (12).
